Both answers tighten the design nicely. Here's the refined shape:

## Updated shape

**Discovery → interactive project picker.** Scan all of `~/.claude/projects/`, then present a multiselect TUI — and you already have the pieces for this (`tui.rs` + the `crossterm` dep). Each row should carry the decision-relevant numbers so you're picking with cost in view, not blind:

```
proactive-context archeologist        # opens the picker

  [x] ~/src/proactive-context     142 sessions   Nov–May   ~118 after triage   ~$3.10
  [x] ~/src/pc-wikitest            38 sessions   Apr–May    ~31 after triage   ~$0.80
  [ ] ~/src/some-throwaway          9 sessions   Jan         ~2 after triage   ~$0.05
  ...
  [enter] mine selected   [a] all   [d] dry-run selected   [q] quit
```

Triage runs *before* the picker so the "after triage" and cost columns are real, not guesses — that's the dry-run estimate baked into the selection screen. `is_already_captured` means rows show only *new* work on re-runs (a project fully mined shows `0 new`).

**Periodic synthesis checkpoints.** Per selected project: replay its sessions oldest→newest, and every K sessions (default maybe 10–15, `--synth-every K`) run a synthesis pass so the accumulating PRODUCT_MODEL self-corrects mid-history — an early "we use npm" gets superseded by a later "switched to bun" *during* the run, not only at the end. Final partial batch always gets a closing synthesis. Cost is bounded by `ceil(sessions/K)` synthesis calls per project, which you can see from the picker counts.

The two load-bearing correctness rules still hold and are the main *new* code:
- **Global chronological sort** within each project before replay (supersession depends on it).
- **Stamp `verified:` from each transcript's own timestamp**, not `today()` — thread the session date through `CaptureContext.today` (the field's already parameterizable; the default path just hardcodes `today()`).

Everything else — routing, dedup/resume, triage, locking — reuses existing functions.

## Live run-view TUI — shipped

**Three-thread design** (per spec): a capture-replay worker, an `events.jsonl` tailer (filtered to the run's projects *and* a run-start time window so months of history don't pollute the feed), and a ratatui main thread that folds events into the dashboard. All the regions from the mockup are there — overall + per-project progress bars, ETA, cost/token ticker, the two-row counters, and the **scrolling live fact-feed** (newest at bottom, supersessions highlighted magenta, `p` to pause, ↑/↓ scroll).

**Keyed off the real v0.4 events** (`wiki.create/add/revise/remove/link`, `capture.triage/done`, `error`) — the dead v0.3 names are explicitly *not* wired, with a regression test proving it.

**Both fixes landed too:** the captured-counter now derives the true captured / triage-skip / too-short split from events (not "every Ok() = captured"), and `transcript_message_count` is now a substring scan — full-backlog picker scan clocks ~3.6s over 1,400 files / 1.3GB.

**Build/tests:** clean build, clippy clean on the new code, **53 tests pass** (5 new).

## The one honest caveat

The TUI **rendering itself hasn't been run live** — the agent's harness is non-TTY, so the dashboard is compile- and inspection-validated only. What *was* validated, for free ($0, zero network calls): the worker machinery end-to-end against an all-too-short project, the real `events.jsonl` parsing cleanly through the tailer, and the TTY gate (piped → line-log, never the TUI). Also: `s` skip-session isn't implemented — capture is one blocking call with a 300s internal timeout, so it's `q`-only (quit finishes the current session first, with a notice so it doesn't look hung).

So the thing for **you** to do is exercise it in a real terminal. Cheapest path to see the live feed actually flow:

```
cargo run -- archeologist            # picker (free) — or...
cargo run -- archeologist --project ~/src/pc-wikitest   # one real (paid) project
```

All four of your asks are in (built clean, worktree `worktree-agent-a661dfd2a1daf2905`):

## 1 & 2 & 3 — picker improvements
- **Sorted by session count** (most-active first, name as tiebreak) — in both the picker and the dry-run table.
- **`/` fuzzy search** — press `/`, type to filter (case-insensitive subsequence match on name + full path), Enter applies / Esc cancels; with a filter active, `a`/`n` select-all/none operate on the *visible* set, and Esc clears the filter before quitting. Matches across all rows of a project.
- **Models shown** — picker header now reads `capture: <model>  triage: <model>`, and the live run-view's Cost line shows `model <capture_model>`. Both pulled from your config (`capture_model` / `capture_triage_model`).

Caveat as before: the TUI changes are compile-validated, not run live (this harness is non-TTY) — worth a real-terminal pass.

## 4 — the "almost empty" projects

**No, there's no limit.** It scans every `.jsonl` with no cap — counts run up to 235. The undercount you're seeing is a *grouping* effect, not a missing-data one. I ran the real binary against your `~/.claude`:

```
podcast-player  227    win  197    nostr-multi-platform 184
TENEX-TUI...    147    TENEX-ff3ssq 126    app 235 (path gone)
```

Projects are grouped by the **exact working directory you launched `claude` from** (the `cwd` inside each transcript, normalized via `canonicalize`). So a project looks sparse when its sessions are spread across *several* cwds:

- **Launched from subdirectories** → split rows. Your `highlighter` is literally three rows: `highlighter` (79) + `highlighter/app/ios/Highlighter` (18) + `highlighter/web` (8). Each subdir you `cd`'d into and ran Claude from is its own row.
- **Project moved/renamed** → old path becomes its own row (often `exists=False`, e.g. `/Users/pablofernandez/src/app` = 235 sessions, dir now gone).
- **Display name is just the basename** → big projects hide under generic names (`app`, `win`, `web`, `main`), so you may not recognize them.

The new sort floats the busy ones to the top, and `/` search lets you pull every fragment of a project together (type `highlighter` → all three rows appear, select them all with `a`).

If you'd rather **one logical project = one row**, I can add a coarser grouping — e.g. collapse sub-paths under their git-repo root, or group by nearest ancestor that contains a `.git`. That's a small follow-up; want it?

## What happened
The "merge" turned out to be a cherry-pick, not a branch merge: the worktree branch had **no unique commits** (its tip was already an ancestor of master), and all the archeologist code was sitting uncommitted in the worktree on a now-stale base. Meanwhile master had moved well ahead (removed `generate`, db fixes, `inject_model` removal, multi-provider).

So I:
1. Committed the archeologist work in the worktree (`eb31b74`).
2. Cherry-picked it onto master → **`5217614`**. Clean, no conflicts — master had left `capture.rs` untouched since the base, and `main.rs`/`transcript.rs` auto-merged.
3. **Verified it builds and runs on master** (`cargo build` clean; dry-run on this project works: 32 sessions / 21 new / ~$30–39).

## Preserved / untouched
- The unrelated WIP on master (`daemon.rs`, `inject.rs` modified, `index.db` untracked) is **untouched** — the cherry-pick only added the 4 archeologist files.
- The spec (`docs/product-spec/archeologist.md`) was already tracked in master.

## One cleanup note
The agent worktree is **harness-locked** (its process still holds the lock), so I couldn't remove it or delete the redundant branch — and I didn't force it, since the harness owns those locks. It's harmless and the harness will reclaim it; the merged code doesn't depend on it.
